summary: The author argues that making it easy for people to do the right thing is more effective than enforcement or intimidation. Drawing from her experience as a self-appointed 'build cop' on a 40+ person team, she learned that nagging developers about broken builds was ineffective when the test suite took 4+ hours to run. By investing in infrastructure improvements—parallel tests, remote execution, in-memory databases—compliance naturally increased. She extends this principle to API design, warning against REST purism that prioritizes computer-brain logic over human readability and error prevention.
key_points: Intimidation and enforcement have limited effectiveness when the 'right thing' is genuinely difficult to do
Investing in making correct behavior easier yields better results than policing incorrect behavior
REST purity and similar standards can make systems harder for humans to use correctly when taken to extremes
Making things 'easy' doesn't mean removing all complexity—it means ensuring the easy path leads to understanding and correct usage
Human nature should be factored into system and process design, not fought against
money_quotes: One of my overriding principles is: make it easy for people to do the right thing.
People were not malicious, but the tests took a LONG time to run (upwards of 4 hours at the worst), and on the older desktops you couldn't even get much work done while the test suite ran.
All my intimidation and bullying wasn't going to cause perfect compliance.
Any quest for purity that ignores human readability does so at its peril.
I generally prefer to use frameworks that force me and my team to do more implementation work rather than making it trivially easy.
Many great engineers are tripped up by human nature, when there's really no need to be.
Look at your critical human-involving processes and think: am I making it easy for people to do the right thing here?
key_insight: Systems and processes should be designed around human nature rather than expecting people to overcome friction through willpower—invest in making the right path the easy path.
